html_002: 1% a day — the science of change that compounds
html_004: “You don’t rise to the level of your goals.
html_005: You fall to the level of your systems.”

.
        You start with the easy ones and it gets harder,
html_010: and any question you get wrong comes back until you get it right.

html_043: and you’ll never forget it. It all starts with

js_009: Written by James Clear in 2018. The original title is Atomic Habits, and it became the world’s habit bible.
js_011: In a word, what size of change does this book champion?
js_013: One huge resolution that flips your life
js_014: Several times more effort than others
js_016: The key is a small 1% change. Those tiny improvements compound into astonishing results.
js_018: If you get 1% better every day, roughly how many times better are you after a year?
js_023: Multiply 1.01 by itself 365 times and you get about 37.8×. Go the other way — 1% worse daily — and it converges to almost zero. Habits are compound interest.
js_025: What does Clear say matters more than goals?
js_026: The system that leads to the goal
js_027: A big, grand goal everyone admires
js_030: “You don’t rise to the level of your goals — you fall to the level of your systems.” It’s not the outcome but the design of the process that matters.
js_032: What motivation does Clear say keeps a habit going the longest?
js_033: The identity of who you want to be
js_037: Not “I’m someone resisting a cigarette” but “I’m a non-smoker” — habits flow from identity.
js_039: What’s the correct order of the 4 stages every habit goes through?
js_044: A habit runs on the loop of cue · craving · response · reward. These 4 stages are the backbone of the 4 Laws of Behavior Change.
js_046: How many laws of behavior change does Clear lay out?
js_051: Make it obvious · attractive · easy · satisfying — these four laws are the framework for building good habits.
js_053: What does Clear suggest when you start a new habit?
js_054: Start so small you can finish in 2 minutes
js_055: Push hard and perfectly from day one
js_056: Set a harder goal than everyone else
js_057: Only do it when you feel like it
js_058: The 2-minute rule — not “read a whole book” but “read one page” first. Lower the barrier to starting and the habit sticks.
js_060: What’s the technique of attaching a new habit to an existing one called?
js_064: The habit stacking formula = “After [current habit], I will [new habit].” It uses an existing habit as the cue.
js_066: What does Clear say drives behavior more than willpower?
js_067: Designing an environment that guides behavior
js_069: Firm resolve you can summon anytime you decide to
js_071: People respond to their environment, not their willpower. Make good habits visible and bad ones invisible.
js_074: James Clear talk · “1% Better Every Day”
js_075: James Clear is an American author and speaker. He studied the power of habits from his own recovery after a high-school baseball injury.
js_076: This book isn’t about ‘bigger’ — it’s about ‘smaller, and more consistent’. The core idea is that a tiny 1% compounds.
js_077: 1.01 to the 365th power is about 37.8; 0.99 to the 365th is about 0.03. Small repetitions in the same direction, met with time, split the results wide apart.
js_078: Goals give you direction; systems make progress. Clear insists you ‘focus on systems, not goals’.
js_079: Every action is a vote for an identity. Not ‘I want to lose weight’ but the belief ‘I’m a person who lives healthily’ is what sustains a habit.
js_080: Cue (trigger) → Craving (motive) → Response (action) → Reward (outcome). Understand this loop and you see how to build and break habits.
js_081: The 4 Laws = obvious · attractive · easy · satisfying. Switch these four on for good habits and off for bad ones.
js_082: The trick is a size ‘too small to skip’. Just start, and momentum carries it on.
js_083: Habit stacking recycles an already-solid habit as the ‘cue’. It rides a new habit on an already-automated flow from the start.
js_084: Willpower is a limited resource. Change your environment and the good choice becomes the default — no gritting your teeth every time.
js_096: 1% better each day becomes about 37× in a year. Small, unnoticed changes compound and decide the outcome.
js_098: You don’t fall to the level of your goals — you fall to the level of your systems. What makes the outcome is the process you repeat daily.
js_100: Of the three layers — outcome, process, identity — identity runs deepest. Decide who you want to be, then prove it through action.
js_102: Cue → Craving → Response → Reward. It’s the circuit every habit runs on. Know the loop and you see how to build and break them.
js_104: Make it obvious · attractive · easy · satisfying. Turn these four on for good habits, off for bad ones.
js_105: Start a new habit small enough to finish in 2 minutes. Showing up comes before finishing.
js_106: After [current habit], do [new habit]. Use an already-automated habit as the cue and stack the new one on top.
js_108: Results don’t track effort in a straight line — they break through at a threshold. Like ice melting at 0°C, you have to endure the plateau.
js_110: The dramatic change tiny habits create
js_111: Author James Clear was struck in the face by a baseball bat in high school and fell into despair.
js_112: Not through grand resolutions but by stacking tiny habits one by one, he recovered and grew into a top player.
js_113: Core message — success comes not from one big leap but from a small 1% repeated daily.
js_114: The question running through the whole book: who am I becoming right now?
js_116: Fundamentals — why tiny habits make a big difference
js_117: 1% better each day is about 37× in a year; 1% worse is nearly zero. Habits work like compound interest.
js_118: Systems, not goals — we fall to the level of our systems, not our goals.
js_119: The most powerful motivation is identity. Start not from ‘what will I achieve’ but ‘who will I become’.
js_120: Every habit runs the loop cue → craving → response → reward. This circuit is the backbone of the 4 Laws.
js_123: Habits start from visible cues. Design your environment to make good cues obvious.
js_124: Implementation intention — nail down the when and where: ‘I will [action] at [time] in [place]’.
js_125: Habit stacking — put a new habit right after a solid one and use it as an automatic cue.
js_126: For bad habits, the most effective move is to erase the cue and make them ‘invisible’.
js_129: Craving pulls action. The more attractive a habit, the more you want to do it.
js_130: Temptation bundling — attach something you want to do right after something you need to do, using motivation as leverage.
js_131: Dopamine surges from ‘anticipation’ more than reward. Design anticipation and craving grows.
js_132: We come to resemble the habits of the groups around us. Join a tribe where your desired habit is the norm.
js_135: What builds a habit isn’t perfection but the number of reps. Cut the friction and make it easy.
js_136: The 2-minute rule — make a new habit small enough to finish in 2 minutes. Lower the barrier to starting.
js_137: Prep your environment in advance: put good actions two steps closer and bad ones farther away.
js_138: Automate the decisive moment (auto-transfers, subscriptions) so one choice locks in the habit.
js_141: Actions that give immediate satisfaction get repeated. Add a small instant reward to good habits.
js_142: Mark the days you did it with a habit tracker — ‘don’t break the chain’ becomes powerful motivation.
js_143: Use habit contracts and accountability partners to attach an immediate cost to failure and curb bad habits.
js_144: The Goldilocks rule — motivation to keep going peaks at the edge of your ability, at just-right difficulty.
js_146: Once bottom of the pack, the British cycling team improved every factor by 1% under coach Dave Brailsford — saddles, sleep, even how they washed their hands. Those small gains piled up and, within a few years, they swept the Olympics and the Tour de France.
js_147: It’s a real-world demonstration of how the sum of small, invisible gains grows into overwhelming results.
js_149: Ice seems unchanged from −5°C to −1°C, then the moment it hits 0°C it starts to melt. Every degree of warming before that wasn’t wasted — it was accumulation toward the threshold.
js_150: It intuitively explains why you must endure the ‘plateau of latent potential’ where effort shows no results yet.
js_152: James Clear was badly hurt when a bat hit his face during a high-school baseball game, but instead of giving up he steadily built small habits of sleep, study and training over several years — growing into his college’s top player and student.
js_153: He personally proves the book’s message: it’s not a dramatic turnaround but daily small repetition that changes a person.
js_154: Am I clinging only to a goal, or do I have a ‘system’ I repeat every day?
js_155: Today, swap your goal for just one small habit you can start in under 2 minutes.
js_156: Who do I want to become, and do today’s habits prove I’m that person?
js_157: Finish the sentence ‘I’m the kind of person who…’, then cast one vote for that identity with a single small action.
js_158: Does my environment make good habits visible and bad ones invisible?
js_159: Reorganize your desk, fridge and phone screen through the lens of ‘habit cues’.
js_163: You’ve nailed almost all the core principles
js_165: You’ve got the framework — one more round!
js_167: The more you repeat, the higher your score
